title: Sir Nicholas L'Estrange, 1st Baronet

infobox.value: **[Baronet](/wiki/L%27Estrange_baronets "L'Estrange baronets")** (of Hunstanton) 1629–1655 | Succeeded by Hamon L'Estrange

From Wikipedia, the free encyclopedia

English baronet and collector of anecdotes

For other people named Nicholas L'Estrange, see [Nicholas L'Estrange (disambiguation)](/wiki/Nicholas_L%27Estrange_(disambiguation) "Nicholas L'Estrange (disambiguation)").

**Sir Nicholas L'Estrange, 1st Baronet** (March 1604 – 24 July 1655) was an English [baronet](/wiki/Baronet "Baronet") and collector of anecdotes.[[1]](#cite_note-ODNB-1)[[2]](#cite_note-DNB-2)

## Biography

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Sir_Nicholas_L%27Estrange,_1st_Baronet&action=edit&section=1 "Edit section: Biography")]

L'Estrange was the third but eldest surviving son of [Sir Hamon le Strange](/wiki/Hamon_le_Strange "Hamon le Strange") and [Alice Stubbe](/wiki/Alice_L%27Estrange "Alice L'Estrange").[[2]](#cite_note-DNB-2) He was raised at [Hunstanton Hall](/wiki/Hunstanton_Hall "Hunstanton Hall") before attending [Trinity College, Cambridge](/wiki/Trinity_College,_Cambridge "Trinity College, Cambridge") in 1622. Two years later he was admitted to [Lincoln's Inn](/wiki/Lincoln%27s_Inn "Lincoln's Inn"). In 1629, his father purchased a baronetcy in the [Baronetage of England](/wiki/Baronetage_of_England "Baronetage of England") for him at a cost of £300, plus £100 in charges.[[3]](#cite_note-3) On 26 August 1630 he married Anne (1612–1663), daughter of Sir Edward Lewkenor of [Denham, West Suffolk](/wiki/Denham,_West_Suffolk "Denham, West Suffolk"). They had eight sons and three daughters.[[1]](#cite_note-ODNB-1)

L'Estrange shared the [Royalist](/wiki/Cavalier "Cavalier") views of his family, but there is no record of him taking an active part in the [English Civil War](/wiki/English_Civil_War "English Civil War"). He declined to attend an October 1642 meeting of militia commanders in Norwich summoned by the [Roundhead](/wiki/Roundhead "Roundhead") authorities. Following this, the Norfolk parliamentary commanders ordered that his house should be searched and any arms removed. He managed to avoid the seizure of his estates under the [Commonwealth of England](/wiki/Commonwealth_of_England "Commonwealth of England") and died on 24 July 1655 at Hunstanton, where he was buried.[[1]](#cite_note-ODNB-1) He was succeeded in his title by his son, Hamon.[[2]](#cite_note-DNB-2)

### Collector of anecdotes

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Sir_Nicholas_L%27Estrange,_1st_Baronet&action=edit&section=2 "Edit section: Collector of anecdotes")]

L'Estrange played an active role in [Norfolk](/wiki/Norfolk "Norfolk") society as a senior member of the county's [gentry](/wiki/Gentry "Gentry") and was often present at the assizes and parliamentary elections at [Norwich](/wiki/Norwich "Norwich"), as well as taking part in hunts and hawking sessions. He was an avid observer and recorder of these interactions, and collected over 600 anecdotes which were published anonymously in *Merry Passages and Jests*.[[4]](#cite_note-4) Many of the anecdotes were so coarse that when the collection was republished by the [Camden Society](/wiki/Camden_Society "Camden Society") as *Anecdotes and Traditions* in 1839, only 141 were deemed suitable to be printed.[[1]](#cite_note-ODNB-1)[[2]](#cite_note-DNB-2)

The attributions reveal his connections across [East Anglian](/wiki/East_Anglian "East Anglian") society, including observations on figures such as [Sir Drue Drury](/wiki/Sir_Drue_Drury,_1st_Baronet "Sir Drue Drury, 1st Baronet"), [Clement Spelman](/w/index.php?title=Clement_Spelman&action=edit&redlink=1 "Clement Spelman (page does not exist)"), [Sir John Hobart](/wiki/Sir_John_Hobart,_2nd_Baronet "Sir John Hobart, 2nd Baronet"), [Miles Hobart](/wiki/Miles_Hobart "Miles Hobart"), and his relations [Hamon L'Estrange](/wiki/Hamon_L%27Estrange "Hamon L'Estrange"), [Roger L'Estrange](/wiki/Roger_L%27Estrange "Roger L'Estrange") and [Sir William Spring](/wiki/Sir_William_Spring,_1st_Baronet "Sir William Spring, 1st Baronet"). The anecdotes present an unparalleled view of provincial life during the period and of the interaction between members of the gentry, as well as of the gossip of the taverns.[[1]](#cite_note-ODNB-1)
